figures.caption: Table 1 Comparison with recent medical video benchmarks.
Table 4 Source-dataset documentation. “Release handling” describes how the benchmark package respects upstream asset terms.

Figure 1 MedHorizon targets the distinctive evidence structure of long medical videos. (a) Unlike many natural videos, where a few discriminative moments can answer a query, medical procedure videos contain highly redundant frames and weak evidence distributed over time. (b) This evidence is extremely sparse: MedHorizon contains 1.7 evidence frames per 1,000 video frames on average, with a mean question-level evidence ratio of 0.166%. (c) MedHorizon evaluates full-procedure retrieval and multi-hop clinical reasoning rather than pre-cut clip QA Su et al. (2026).

Table 2 Main results on MedHorizon. Input reports the visual input protocol: a number indicates a fixed uniform sampled frame budget, while “1 FPS” indicates fps-based video sampling. Best results are in bold. Darker shades indicate stronger values within each column.
Figure 10 Frame-change sampling helps short-window tasks, but uniform sampling remains the most stable overall strategy. And other generic sampling methods also have limited improvement.
Table 3 Benchmark card summary for the final evaluation artifact.
Table 7 Open-source, specialized long-video, and medical-model protocol. Fixed-frame rows use uniform sampling over the full source video unless the method is an adaptive selector.

Figure 7 Diagnostic breakdown under four input settings: random guessing, text-only questions, uniformly sampled full-video frames, and oracle GT-images. The comparison separates language shortcuts, temporal evidence retrieval, and post-retrieval evidence interpretation.
Figure 3 Overview of the source corpus.
Figure 8 Disrupting event order consistently hurts performance. That there is only a slight performance decrease underscores the models’ inability to effectively exploit temporal information.
Figure 9 Attention distribution and similarity in frame and patch-level.
Figure 4 Representative video-question pairs across fine-grained understanding and semantic reasoning tasks in MedHorizon.
